examples: That's the most cockamamie plan I've ever heard — and I once watched a man try to open a jar with his elbow for twenty minutes.
His cockamamie theories about pigeons running the government somehow got more convincing the longer he talked.
vocab.notes: Means absurd, implausible, poorly thought out. Perfect for dismissing nonsense with style.

examples: He walks into every meeting like a prancing pony — all confidence, no destination.
I told him to relax and he hit me with the full prancing pony entrance.
vocab.notes: Someone who moves or behaves with exaggerated self-importance or showmanship. High-step energy.

examples: He bloviates for forty minutes and somehow says less than a fortune cookie.
I don't mind public speakers. I mind people who bloviate while looking directly into your soul.
vocab.notes: To talk at length in a pompous or empty way. The sound of the word does half the work.

examples: She's an absolute flibbertigibbet — three conversations at once, none of them finished.
I was trying to explain the situation but he went full flibbertigibbet on me.
vocab.notes: A frivolous, flighty, or excessively talkative person. Maximum silliness per syllable ratio.

examples: He came in with some mighty strong opinions for someone who just discovered the topic yesterday.
That's a mighty bold claim from a man eating a gas station sandwich.
vocab.notes: Conveys scale, force, or audacity. Works deadpan — undersell the delivery, let the word carry weight.

examples: Whatcha mean the restaurant closed? I made a reservation two years ago.
Whatcha mean that's not how taxes work? I've been filing like this for a decade.
vocab.notes: Expresses genuine or performative disbelief. Works best with a pause before it. Streetwise, reactive energy.

examples: He walked in big dick swinging like he invented the concept of a Monday meeting.
There's a certain kind of consultant who comes in big dick swinging and leaves with your budget and your dignity.
vocab.notes: Walking into a situation with maximum unearned confidence. The audacity of presence without credentials.

examples: My nephew is a lil gremlin baby — adorable, chaotic, no rules apply to him.
3am and my brain is being a lil gremlin baby again, serving up every embarrassing memory from 2009.
vocab.notes: A small creature operating outside of normal social contracts. Term of chaotic affection. Also works for yourself at your worst.

examples: They redesigned the app and now it's a grotesque monstrosity of forty tabs and no back button.
I made a smoothie with whatever was in the fridge. It was a grotesque monstrosity. I drank it twice.
vocab.notes: Maximum dramatic condemnation. The gap between the word and what it's applied to is the joke. Use on small things.

examples: The slideshow was pure eye candy — beautiful charts, zero information.
He described himself as eye candy. I appreciated his commitment to the bit.
vocab.notes: Visually appealing, often with the implication that substance is absent. Can be affectionate or cutting.

examples: The menu description was mouth watering. The actual dish was a beige situation.
He described his plan with mouth watering detail, then revealed the budget was eleven dollars.
vocab.notes: Works best when the reality undercuts the hype. Build up the anticipation then land flat.

examples: We finally got the printer to work. Huzzah. Huzzah I say.
Huzzah — the meeting ended four minutes early. Mark this day in the chronicles.
vocab.notes: Archaic cheer. Deeply ironic when applied to modern mundane victories. Medieval energy for 21st century nothing.

examples: I found a twenty in my old jacket. Booyah. That's a retirement plan right there.
Booyah — I remembered to hydrate before noon. Personal record.
vocab.notes: 90s victory call. Ironic sincerity is the move — deliver it like you mean it on something small.

examples: He asked me to pass the salt. "Of course, ur majesty." He didn't like that.
She sent a follow-up email to her follow-up email. Ur majesty is not to be kept waiting.
vocab.notes: Sarcastic deference to someone acting royally entitled. The "ur" keeps it modern, the "majesty" lands the hit.

examples: He described his dating profile strategy. Boo, the ewww.
The catering served warm potato salad. Boo. The ewww.
vocab.notes: Double condemnation — the boo and the ewww hit from different angles. Disgust plus rejection. Works as a complete sentence.

examples: After six weeks of "treating myself" I emerged a fattyboomflabby triumphant.
He said the word fattyboomflabby in a pitch meeting and somehow got the contract.
vocab.notes: Pure phonetic delight. Affectionate self-destruction. The word itself sounds like what it describes — maximum wobble energy.

examples: We're not influencers. We're fattyboom baddies and that's a different category entirely.
The fattyboom baddies have arrived and we are not accepting feedback at this time.
vocab.notes: Reclaimed confidence. Size positivity with swag. Works as a collective noun — solidarity energy.

examples: She moved into what she describes as a big boobie bungalow. Three bedrooms, good light, correct priorities.
The airbnb listing said cozy cottage. Reader, it was a big boobie bungalow.
vocab.notes: Architecture meets body positivity meets absurdism. The specificity of "bungalow" makes it. Suburban, cheerful, unashamed.

vocab.word: Lube for the cog in the sexmachine
examples: He positions himself as the lube for the cog in the sexmachine of this organisation. HR has thoughts.
Every team needs a vibe person. The lube for the cog in the sexmachine. I have volunteered.
vocab.notes: Mechanical metaphor goes full blue. Works as a role description. Say it with complete professionalism and watch the room react.

examples: Woe betide the businessman who assumes the room agrees with him just because no one spoke up.
Woe betide anyone who touches my leftovers. This is not a joke. This is a warning.
vocab.notes: Old English formal curse structure. The contrast between the archaic phrasing and the modern grievance is the engine. Great for elevated petty.

examples: The 6am email senders are ghouls. Every single one of them.
The ghouls are at the networking event again, wearing lanyards and asking about synergies.
vocab.notes: Anyone operating outside the normal human contract — usually corporate, bureaucratic, or chronologically unacceptable. Said with weary recognition.

examples: He prescribed himself a shortstack remedy — pancakes, maple syrup, zero accountability.
The cure for a bad week is a shortstack remedy. Dense, warm, unreasonably effective.
vocab.notes: A small but potent fix. The shortstack is deceptively filling — compact solution with outsized results. Works for any low-effort high-impact solution.

examples: He arrived at the meeting like a dildo ramjet — fast, impractical, and completely unrequested.
The proposal went through the boardroom like a dildo ramjet. Nobody stopped it. Nobody knew how.
vocab.notes: Unstoppable forward momentum combined with questionable utility. Something that barrels through opposition purely on kinetic confidence.

examples: He sat in the corner wearing the dunce cap of his own opinions.
Every room has a dunce. Every meeting has two. Every Zoom call has an entire dunce ecosystem.
vocab.notes: Beautifully old-fashioned condemnation. The word itself sounds like the concept — blunt, round, hollow. Deploy with patience.

examples: He approached the situation like a knuckledragging troglodyte and somehow got promoted.
The comment section was a rich ecosystem of knuckledragging troglodytes finding community.
vocab.notes: Maximum evolutionary contempt. The double compound makes it ceremonial — not casual insult but formal indictment. Reserve for deserving targets.

examples: He liked every single one of her posts going back to 2016. Absolute thirst bucket behaviour.
She walked in like a thirst bucket at a hydration convention — everyone noticed, no one was surprised.
vocab.notes: Someone leaking desire from every pore, usually online. The "bucket" framing implies capacity, volume, spillage. Affectionate or devastating depending on delivery.

examples: Stop lollygagging and get in the van.
He spent forty minutes lollygagging in the cereal aisle, weighing his options like a man with nowhere to be and nothing to lose.
vocab.notes: Wasting time in a pleasurable or aimless way. One of the great words — sounds exactly like what it means. Maximum dawdle energy.

examples: She entered the gala like a full tranny boom blammy — the room didn't know what happened but it knew something had.
I asked for subtle. I got tranny boom blammy. I should have been clearer.
vocab.notes: Explosive, gender-chaotic arrival energy. Someone who hits the room with maximum force and no apologies. See also: funwordizer examples.

vocab.word: Ur Chilly So Here's Some Chili
examples: He looked cold and sad so she handed him a bowl and said ur chilly so here's some chili. He cried. It helped.
I don't have emotional support. I have ur chilly so here's some chili. It's the same thing.
vocab.notes: Phonetic swap as act of care. The wrong solution that's somehow exactly right. Warmth delivered incorrectly but sincerely.

examples: They didn't fix the problem, they just gave the whole department a lubebotomy and sent them home.
He came out of the board meeting looking like he'd had a lubebotomy. Technically fine. Fundamentally different.
vocab.notes: Funwordized "lobotomy" — a procedure that fixes things from the wrong end. Medical gravity applied to undignified processes. See funwordizer lane.

examples: He walked in wearing a suit made of leather and I said heilige schnitzel under my breath.
She revealed the final bill. Heilige schnitzel. I sat down.
vocab.notes: German exclamation — literally "holy schnitzel." Delivers the force of a swear word with the absurdity of a breaded veal cutlet. Maximum impact, minimum offense. Works in any language because nobody knows what it means.

examples: The comments section is just hall monitors all the way down.
He's a hall monitor energy type — technically correct, deeply resented, operating on borrowed authority.
vocab.notes: People who enforce rules they didn't make in spaces that don't belong to them, with an enthusiasm that only makes sense if the badge is their whole identity. Works as a noun, adjective, or energy.
